45.1.1.1: A suitably qualified and experienced health professional is identified with clearly defined and documented responsibilities for coordination of the volunteer service.
45.1.1.2: The manager is represented on the volunteers’ management committee to act as liaison between the organisation and the volunteer service.
45.1.1.3: Recorded meetings between the organisation management and volunteers’ management are held at least monthly.
45.1.1.4: There is a current organisational chart, which clearly states the agreed upon lines of communication, authority, responsibility and accountability.
45.1.1.5: There is evidence that volunteers are selected in accordance with the necessary and appropriate skills to support the objectives of the volunteer service.
45.1.1.6: Volunteers are clearly identified by name badges.
45.1.1.7: Personnel employed in the volunteer service are assigned based on qualification, experience and interest.
45.1.1.8: There is a mechanism to ensure that all volunteers work under the guidance of suitably qualified health professionals in the employ of the organisation.
45.1.1.9: The volunteer service is supported by administrative, auxiliary and secretarial personnel who may themselves be volunteers.
45.1.1.10: There is a system in place to evaluate the performance of volunteers.
45.1.1.11: There is a system in place to provided acknowledgement and appreciation for services rendered by each volunteer.
45.1.2.1: The agreement includes indemnity and waiver for injuries sustained on the premises.
45.1.2.2: The agreement includes the maintaining of confidentiality and privacy of information.
45.1.2.3: The agreement includes those functions, which may be carried out by volunteers.
45.1.2.4: The agreement includes access to premises and facilities by volunteers.
45.1.2.5: Agreements are regularly reviewed and amended when required according to organisational policy.
45.2.1.1: There is written, planned orientation and induction programme for all new volunteers to the service.
45.2.1.2: The orientation and induction programme introduces new volunteers to the relevant aspects of the volunteer service.
45.2.1.3: The orientation and induction programme explains the relationship and line of communication between the volunteer service and the organisation.
45.2.1.4: The orientation and induction programme prepares volunteers for their roles and responsibilities.
45.2.1.5: The orientation and induction programme introduces volunteers to the policies and procedures of the volunteer service and the organisation.
45.2.1.6: The orientation and induction programme ensures the new volunteers are fully informed of the conditions of confidentiality and conditions of providing information to the media.
45.2.1.7: All volunteers are trained regarding their role during a fire and emergency evacuation.
45.2.1.8: All volunteers are trained regarding their role in providing a safe and secure patient care facility.
45.2.1.9: The orientation and induction programme is reviewed annually, signed and dated.
45.2.2.1: The volunteer service provides a relevant, written in-service training programme for volunteers.
45.2.2.2: There is a system in place to ensure that all volunteers attend in-service training programmes, and that records are kept.
45.2.2.3: The volunteer service ensures that the volunteers are kept up to date with relevant new information.
45.2.2.4: The volunteer service manager ensures that all volunteers are able to initiate basic life support as necessary.
45.2.2.5: The volunteer service manager ensures that all volunteers are familiar with the organisation’s internal and external fire and emergency evacuation plans and attend rehearsals at least annually.
45.2.2.6: The volunteer service manager ensures that all volunteers are trained in the relevant health and safety policies and infection control policies.
45.3.1.1: The volunteer service has written policies and procedures to guide volunteers in the activities and management of the volunteer service.
45.3.1.2: Volunteers are involved in the formulation of policies and procedures as well as protocols for volunteer service.
45.3.1.3: All policies and procedures are reviewed at appropriate intervals, dated and signed.
45.3.1.4: Policies and procedures are kept in a manual, which is indexed and easily accessible.
45.3.1.5: There is a mechanism to ensure that all policies and procedures are known and implemented by all volunteers working in the volunteer service.
45.4.1.1: The volunteer service management personnel are included in the management committee of the organisation to advise on planning for equipment acquisition, deployment, utilisation and maintenance for the volunteer service.
45.4.1.2: Telephones are available for volunteers and visitors.
45.4.1.3: There is an alarm/alert system in place in case of medical emergencies, which is known to all members of the volunteer service.
45.4.1.4: There is an alarm system in place in case of security threats, which is known to all members of the volunteer service.
45.4.1.5: There is office facility available for the volunteer service manager.
45.4.1.6: There is adequate space for patient/group work by the volunteer service.
45.4.1.7: There is adequate light and ventilation in therapy areas.
45.4.1.8: There is adequate and suitable equipment to provide appropriate stimulation to the patients.
45.4.1.9: There is adequate storage space for all equipment, appliances and tools.
45.4.1.10: All furnishing and equipment are clean and in good condition to ensure the safety of patients and volunteers.
45.5.1.1: The functions of the volunteers relating to patient care are clearly defined and supervised.
45.5.1.2: The volunteer services bring specific matters relating to the health of patients to the attention of the medical and/or nursing personnel.
45.5.1.3: Reports on volunteer involvement in group and individual resident stimulation are provided by volunteers to support further clinical decisions.
45.5.1.4: Reporting on patient progress to the multidisciplinary team is done on a regular basis.
45.6.1.1: There is a written quality improvement programme for the volunteer service that is developed and agreed upon by the personnel of the service.
45.6.1.2: Indicators of performance are identified to evaluate the quality of treatment and patient care.
45.6.1.3: Processes are selected in order of priority for evaluation and improvement in the quality of treatment and care.
45.6.1.4: The quality improvement cycle includes the monitoring and evaluation of the standards set, and remedial action implemented.
45.6.1.5: A documentation audit system is in place.
45.6.1.6: An incident managing system for monitoring near misses/adverse events/sentinel events is implemented, which includes the documentation of responses to recorded incidents and interventions to prevent recurrence of the incident or minimise harm in the event of a recurrence.
45.7.1.1: There are processes that support patient and family rights during volunteer care.
45.7.1.2: There are processes to ensure that volunteer care is respectful of the patient’s personal values and beliefs.
45.7.1.3: Measures are taken to protect the patient’s privacy, person and possessions.
45.7.1.4: Volunteers respect the rights of patients and families to treatment and to refuse treatment.
45.8.1.1: The organisation identifies the procedures and processes associated with the risk of infection during volunteer services and implements strategies to reduce risk.
45.8.1.2: The organisation provides education on infection control practices to volunteers.
45.8.1.3: Volunteers are trained in correct hand washing procedures.
45.8.1.4: Organisational policy on handling, storage and disposal of healthcare waste is implemented.
